title: 🎙️ Indigenous Lands & Innovation
description: Today we explore how Cariboo and BC Indigenous nations are using technology on their own terms — for language revitalization, land stewardship, cultural preservation, and self-determined governance. We're looking for stories that centre Indigenous voices and leadership in innovation, where technology serves community sovereignty rather than replacing it. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we explore how Cariboo and BC Indigenous nations are using technology on their own terms — for language revitalization, land stewardship, cultural preservation, and self-determined governance. We're looking for stories that centre Indigenous voices and leadership in innovation, where technology serves community sovereignty rather than replacing it.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Indigenous Lands & Innovation' — Indigenous nations using technology, governance, and stewardship on their own terms.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Indigenous-led journalism on governance, rights, and self-determination should score in the moderate-to-good range even without an explicit technology angle — do not collapse everything that lacks a 'tech' hook to near 0:
- 70-100: Cariboo-specific stories bridging Indigenous governance/culture AND technology — Tŝilhqot'in/Secwépemc/Dakelh language revitalization tech, guardian programs using sensors/drones, GIS land mapping, Indigenous-led connectivity, digital cultural heritage projects.
- 45-69: Pan-Canadian Indigenous governance, sovereignty, land rights, treaty, or reconciliation journalism — APTN, Eagle Feather News, or IndigiNews-style coverage of First Nations governance, MMIWG, land claims, self-determination, or Indigenous-led services — even without a technology angle, per the curator's standing interest in Indigenous governance and sovereignty.
- 25-44: Indigenous cultural, community, or human-interest stories (awards, events, ceremonies) with limited governance or tech substance, or BC land-stewardship/environmental stories with meaningful First Nations involvement.
- 10-24: General BC land, environmental, or forestry stories that mention Indigenous communities only in passing.
- 0-9: No Indigenous angle at all.

Strong fits include: language revitalization technology for Tŝilhqot'in/Secwépemc/Dakelh, Indigenous guardian programs using remote monitoring and sensors, land management tech and environmental monitoring on Indigenous lands, digital tools for traditional knowledge preservation, community mapping and GIS for land claims, Indigenous-led connectivity projects, technology for cultural heritage documentation, mobile apps for language learning, drone/satellite monitoring for stewardship, reconciliation through digital collaboration, how Cariboo Indigenous nations use innovation for self-determination, and Indigenous governance, sovereignty, treaty, and land-rights journalism from any Canadian nation. Prefer articles that bridge Indigenous governance/culture AND technology, but governance/sovereignty journalism alone is a solid fit. Penalise heavily (score 0–9): international geopolitical and war news with no Indigenous rights angle; general wildfire and BC Wildlife Service updates not led by or directly impacting Indigenous communities or guardians; provincial forestry and environmental policy without explicit First Nations involvement; consumer tech and gadget reviews; science discoveries without Indigenous stewardship or sovereignty context; crime and judicial news unrelated to Indigenous rights. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Harvard scientists turn a silicon chip into a DNA writing machine
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

Scientists have created a silicon chip that can write dozens of DNA sequences simultaneously using electricity and water-based enzymes, offering a cleaner alternative to conventional DNA manufacturing. The breakthrough could eventually support portable DNA-writing devices and even massive DNA data storage, although new chemistry will be needed to scale the technology further.
items.summary: Scientists have created a silicon chip that can write dozens of DNA sequences simultaneously using electricity and water-based enzymes, offering a cleaner alternative to conventional DNA manufacturing. The breakthrough could eventually support portable DNA-writing devices and even massive DNA data
items._excerpt: Scientists have created a silicon chip that can write dozens of DNA sequences simultaneously using electricity and water-based enzymes, offering a cleaner alternative to conventional DNA manufacturing. The breakthrough could eventually support portable DNA-writing devices and even massive DNA data storage, although new chemistry will be needed to scale the technology further.

items.title: [Boing Boing] In 1990, engineers wrote an internet standard for carrier pigeons
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

According to IP over Avian Carriers, the internet's standards body once published a straight-faced proposal "to carry Internet Protocol (IP) traffic by birds such as homing pigeons." RFC 1149, written by David Waitzman and released on April 1, 1990, was one of several April Fools' Day standards. — Read the rest

The post In 1990, engineers wrote an internet standard for carrier pigeons appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [New Atlas] Morphing, color-changing liquid stores energy by “charging” into a gel
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

Energy storage usually brings to mind batteries, capacitors, tanks of hydrogen, or maybe some giant gravity system hauling blocks up a tower. Northwestern University researchers have now demonstrated something a lot stranger: a yellow liquid that “charges” by rebuilding itself into a black gel, stores electrons for months, then releases them on demand to drive chemical reactions.

Continue Reading

Category: Energy, Engineering

Tags: Electricity, Northwestern University, Materials

items.title: [Boing Boing] We might be getting that New Vegas followup, but not in the way anyone expected
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

As has been discussed to death both here and on the Internet at large, Fallout: New Vegas is one of the best RPGs ever made. Although it started life as a contracted-out amuse-bouche to keep Fallout fans happy while Bethesda was all hands on deck with Skyrim, Obsidian — an RPG studio formed from the same ex-Black Isle devs who created the series to begin with — knocked it out of the park nonetheless. — Read the rest

The post We might be getting that New Vegas followup, but not in the way anyone expected appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

In a perfect world, the 1967 Outer Space Treaty would be honoured by every nuclear-armed nation in the world, allowing everyone to breath easy about the fact that, of all the ways we humans have sorted out how to kill one another, at least we can rule out atomic fire from beyond our planet's atmosphere (yeah, ballistic missiles get high up there, but work with me here). — Read the rest

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Rare goblin shark filmed alive for the first time in the deep sea
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

For the first time, researchers have filmed the elusive goblin shark alive in the deep ocean where it naturally lives. The remarkable sightings greatly expand the shark's known range and depth, showing that this 125-million-year-old "living fossil" still has plenty of secrets left to reveal.

items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

Poison control calls involving semaglutide (Ozempic and Wegovy) soared after the drug was approved for weight management, with researchers linking the increase to accidental dosing mistakes rather than intentional misuse. Simple education about proper weekly dosing and gradual dose increases could help prevent many of these avoidable incidents.

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] Cancer cases expected to soar worldwide, WHO report finds
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

The disease will touch 92% of people globally, finds annual review, while ‘persistent’ inequities found to exist in access to prevention, diagnosis, treatment and care

Remarkable scientific progress against cancer has changed very little for millions of patients globally, who face devastating physical, emotional and financial consequences after diagnosis, a new World Health Organization report has warned.

One person in five will develop cancer, according to WHO estimates, and the disease will touch 92% of people, either through their own diagnosis or that of a close family member.

Continue reading...

One person in five will develop cancer, according to WHO estimates, and the disease will touch 92% of people, either through their own diagnosis or that of a close family member. Continue

items.title: [Kottke.org] America: Birth Of A Nation
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

To celebrate the 250th anniversary of our great nation, The Onion has produced a Ken Burns-esque film called Birth of a Nation (“the only movie ever named this”).

250 years ago, a group of illiterate men would gather in these hallowed halls to scribble down what historians can now only assume were words. Words that would one day be assigned meaning. Words and pictures. Pictures mostly. That would serve as the founding principles for a grand new experiment that would forever change the course of human history. An experiment that would produce a monstrosity so powerful it would soon be known to the whole world by just one name. America.

Tags: politics · The Onion · usa · video
